nativeRange.habitat: Deep lagoon and seaward reefs; forms mid-water aggregations along steep slopes at depths of approximately 1–50 m

diet.notes: In the wild, the Vlamingii Tang feeds primarily on zooplankton in open water, but in captivity it is most commonly maintained with a diet leaning toward plant matter. Dried marine algae sheets (nori), spirulina-based preparations, and macroalgae offered on a veggie clip at least three times per week form the dietary foundation, and meaty foods such as mysis and enriched brine shrimp are generally accepted alongside these. A vegetable-heavy diet is commonly considered important for tang health, immune function, and prevention of Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE — a condition that causes pitting and discoloration along the face and lateral line).

summary: A large, active Indo-Pacific surgeonfish with a pronounced rounded snout and electric-blue vertical markings that adults can flash or conceal. Generally considered peaceful toward most reef fish but has been known to be aggressive toward other tangs. Best suited to very large aquariums with long swim corridors and a secure lid.
careNotes:
The Vlamingii Tang is among the larger surgeonfishes kept in the aquarium hobby and is generally considered to need substantial swimming space. Tank length tends to matter more than overall volume — an 8-foot (240 cm) footprint is widely cited as a practical minimum for long-term care.

A tight-fitting lid is strongly advised. This species is prone to jumping and has been reported to escape even through small gaps in aquarium covers.

Like all Naso tangs, the Vlamingii Tang bears a pair of sharp, permanently erect spines on either side of the caudal peduncle (the narrow section at the base of the tail), often called caudal scalpels. These are not retractable, so care is warranted when netting the fish; injuries to both handlers and tankmates have been reported.

Coloration changes substantially with age. Juveniles tend to be lighter along the back and darker toward the belly, with relatively muted patterning. Adults develop a brown-to-purple body with vivid blue facial and fin highlights and rows of vertical blue markings that can be flashed during display or muted at rest. Because of this divergence between juvenile and adult appearance, young specimens are sometimes sold without obvious resemblance to the mature fish they will become.

In the wild this species feeds mainly on zooplankton in open water, but captive individuals are widely maintained with a plant-heavy diet — primarily dried marine algae and spirulina — supplemented with meaty foods. An algae-rich diet is commonly considered important for long-term health, color maintenance, and prevention of Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE).

The Vlamingii Tang is broadly considered reef safe and unlikely to harm sessile invertebrates such as corals or anemones.

Conspecific and intra-genus aggression is well documented: Vlamingii Tangs tend to be aggressive toward other tangs — particularly other Naso species and conspecifics — while generally remaining peaceful toward unrelated reef fish. Keeping pairs or small groups is considered realistic only in very large systems.

Like most surgeonfishes, this species is considered susceptible to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE) when kept in poor water quality or with insufficient marine algae in the diet.

Captive breeding has not been documented; pair spawning is observed in the wild.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented for Naso vlamingii. Wild reproduction involves pair spawning along reef slopes. The species is not considered a candidate for hobby breeding attempts.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source. Confirms scientific name, family, max size (24 in / 61 cm), care level (Moderate), temperament (Semi-Aggressive, aggressive toward other tangs but peaceful with other fish), reef safe, minimum tank (360 gal / 1363 L), tight-fitting lid recommended, water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), and herbivore-leaning diet with marine seaweed offered at least 3x weekly.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Acanthuridae / Acanthuriformes / subfamily Nasinae), max length 60 cm TL, max age 45 years, depth range 1–50 m, native distribution (East Africa to Galapagos, north to southern Japan, south to southern GBR/New Caledonia, Tuamotus, Micronesia), deep lagoon and seaward reef habitat forming mid-water aggregations, preferred temperature ~25.5–29°C (mean 27.6°C), and characterizes wild diet as zooplankton feeder (omnivore). IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: Cincinnati Coral and Aquarium
additional.notes: Additional tank-size data point: 240 gallons (910 L) recommended initially with 300+ gallons and an 8-foot footprint long-term. Confirmed semi-aggressive temperament, reef safe, herbivore-leaning diet (nori, pellets, mysis, macroalgae) with small frequent feedings. Used in tank-size averaging and length minimum (240 cm / 8 ft).

additional.name: Top Shelf Aquatics
additional.notes: Lower-end tank-size data point: established 200 gal (~760 L) with 72-inch length minimum. Confirmed reef safe, herbivore diet, peaceful toward most fish but aggressive toward other tangs. Used in tank-size averaging (biased larger per playbook).

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding documented. Wild pair spawning observed along reef slopes. Not considered feasible under hobby conditions.
